headline: EU bans fashion brands from burning unsold clothes
meta_description: Large EU fashion companies can no longer incinerate unsold inventory. The new rule forces brands to sell, discount, or donate instead of destroying millions of…
summary_short: The EU has made it illegal for large fashion companies to incinerate unsold clothing, shoes, and accessories. Companies must now find alternatives like markdowns and cha…
body:
The European Union has made it illegal for large fashion companies to incinerate unsold clothing, shoes, and accessories. As of this week, firms with more than 250 employees and annual revenue over roughly $55 million must find alternatives: markdowns, outlet sales, or charity donations. The prohibition is part of the EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation and represents a fundamental shift in how the fashion industry accounts for its waste. Previously, incineration was simply cheaper than storing goods, managing logistics, or coordinating with charities—so companies did it routinely. The European Environment Agency found that four to nine percent of unsold textile products in the EU are destroyed annually, with online returns making the problem worse: one in five items ordered online gets returned and never resold.

The real story isn't what companies are now forbidden to do—it's what they're forced to reckon with. When disposal is no longer economical, overproduction becomes expensive. Companies that once wrote off incineration as a routine business cost must now absorb storage, logistics, and redistribution expenses themselves. This shifts the entire economic calculus. Large firms must publish annual reports on discarded goods and maintain records for five years, creating transparency that didn't exist before. Medium-sized companies will face the same rules starting in 2030.

The German Retail Federation acknowledged the challenge, noting that not all unsold goods can be easily resold or donated due to damaged packaging and logistics costs. Yet they also recognized a silver lining: shoppers are likely to see more discounted and second-hand clothing available. In essence, the EU has embedded circular economy principles directly into law—making it impossible for fashion companies to ignore the true cost of what they make but don't sell.
quick_facts: Applies to companies with 250+ employees and €50M+ annual revenue
4-9% of unsold textiles in EU destroyed yearly; 20% of online returns never resold
Companies must publish annual discard reports and keep records five years
Medium-sized firms subject to same rules starting 2030
